reason: The prompt rewrite introduces an under-specified severity-loosening clause -- 'allow stepping a single non-essential-control finding down to medium' -- with no definition of what makes a control 'non-essential'. Combined with the unchanged block_threshold=high (a medium does NOT block), this creates a concrete path for a genuine accessibility high (missing alt / div-onClick-as-button / keyboard-inaccessibility) to be silently downgraded to a non-gating medium, i.e. real under-flagging of a WCAG defect class. No fixture exercises this downgrade boundary, so the loosened severity is untested. Compounding the risk: the change ADDS two unproven good+bad pairs (div-onClick-as-button, keyboard-inaccessibility) for the two high categories most likely to be hit by the downgrade clause, yet has zero baseline grading evidence for them -- the only verified data is the single img-no-alt path (bad recall 1.00 high 3/3, good accessible-img fp 0.00 / [] 3/3). The agent is already HEALTHY (recall 1.00 / fp 0.00) with no false-positive problem to solve, so the downgrade clause buys nothing on precision while opening an under-flagging seam. Per the default-to-refuted directive given a concrete, file-verified objection, refute shipping as-is: either drop the 'step down to medium' clause (keep 'otherwise unchanged') or define 'non-essential-control' tightly and add a fixture that pins the medium severity before merging.
